query: recent studies on PRP-40 and its role in microexon splicing

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Mechanism -- Splicing and Pre-mRNA Modifications
retrieved_docs.content: Splicing occurs within the nucleus. Several steps are catalyzed by large (60S) molecules called spliceosomes composed of small ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs) and splicing factors. These enzymes excise the introns from the mRNA transcript while leaving exons in the transcript alone. Spliceosomes shuffle around these exons, depending on the type of protein that needs to be synthesized. After splicing, the 5′ and 3′ tails are modified to help the translation process. At the 5′ end of the pre-mRNA molecule, GTP reacts with the first nucleotide in the triphosphate group on the 5′ ribose's carbon and form a 5′–5′ triphosphate linkage; the nitrogen-7 of guanine is then methylated to form the 5 ′ caps of the mRNA. At the 3′ end, the sequence AAUAAA is cleaved by an endonuclease so that a Poly(A)-polymerase can add the adenylate residues. After all the necessary modifications are made, the mRNA strand is considered mature and is then translocated to the cytoplasm to begin translation.

retrieved_docs.title: Patisiran -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: This RISC separates the strands of the siRNA sequence. One strand releases and the other remains bound to the RISC. Then, the bound strand of siRNA functions as a targeting sequence for the complementary mRNA sequence. The bound patisiran strand binds to the genetically conserved sequence located in the 3' untranslated region of the complementary mRNA encoding abnormal transthyretin and the alignment of the RISC complex with it. [1] Subsequently, transthyretin mRNA is cleaved to render it nonfunctional. The degradation of mRNA coding wild-type and mutant transthyretin, a process of post-transcriptional gene silencing, results in reduced levels of TTR protein in the serum and, ultimately, deposition of TTR protein, also called amyloid, in tissues. [2] [14] One copy of the siRNA target sequence can destroy multiple complementary transthyretin mRNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A major concern is RNA mutations, which can disrupt the normal functioning of RNA and cause potentially life-threatening diseases. RNA errors can be the result of defects in the ribonucleoprotein complex, RNA itself, RNA binding proteins, or any RNA assembly factors. Myotonic dystrophy is a neuromuscular disease that is caused by a CTG nucleotide repeat on the DMPK gene resulting in a pathogenic RNA gain-of-function. [3] A mutation in splicing can result in a mutated SMN2 gene and lead to spinal muscular atrophy. [4] Other concerning illnesses caused by RNA mutations include Prader-Willi syndrome, prostate cancer, fragile X syndrome, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).

retrieved_docs.title: Kuru -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prion proteins (PrP) are encoded by the prion protein gene (PRNP gene). [13] PrPc is a normally folded protein, rich in alpha-helices, and undergoes complete proteolysis. The PrPSc isoform is the misfolded protein that gives rise to the disease; it is rich in beta sheets and is resistant to degradation by enzymes. [14] Thus the PrPSc aggregates and further promotes the conversion of other PrPc to PrPSc. A chain reaction is set, which results in the pathogenesis of prion disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Dystrophinopathies -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: To date, there are several therapies under investigation, from gene repair and therapy to drugs affecting the expression of dystrophin. Eteplirsen is one of the latter. Approved by the FDA for infusion in 2016, it works by skipping exon 51 during pre-mRNA splicing correcting dystrophin expression. [21] Ataluren is another drug on trials, with the goal of bypassing pathogenic variants through promoting ribosomal read-through, therefore continuing dystrophin expression. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several hypotheses surrounding SMN protein and its role in SMA exist, two main hypotheses relate to SMN protein’s role in 1) The neuronal cytoplasm and 2) the neuronal nucleus. SMN protein in the cytoplasm has demonstrated a vital role in mRNA transport through axons, actin dynamics, and vesicle release in the synapse. In the nucleus, SMN protein forms small nuclear RNA’s (snRNA) and therefore plays a key role in the formation of the spliceosome, which removes introns in pre- mRNA into functional mRNA. [14] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- Small Nuclear RNA
retrieved_docs.content: Small nuclear RNAs (snRNA) are non-coding RNAs that are responsible for splicing introns. The snRNAs join with proteins to form small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNP), which most commonly contain U1, U2, U4, U5, and U6 snRNA molecules. [12] Spliceosome assembly and activity begin once U1 of the snRNP binds a complementary sequence on the 5’ splice site of a pre-mRNA transcript. Introns are then removed from the pre-mRNA transcript by the spliceosome complex, and mature mRNA forms.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- MicroRNA
retrieved_docs.content: MicroRNAs (miRNA) are non-coding RNAs mainly involved in gene regulation. They are mostly processed from introns and are transcribed into primary miRNA from the host gene by RNA polymerase II. They are then modified by endonucleases, such as Drosha and Dicer, into mature miRNA. Studies have shown that miRNAs that bind to an untranslated region (3’UTR) on mRNAs suppress translation, while miRNA binding to promoter regions can upregulate transcription. [13] miRNAs can also function similarly to hormones. They are released into the extracellular fluid and taken up by target cells for regulation of cellular activity. Additionally, researchers are studying these extracellular miRNAs as ideal biomarkers for various diseases. Research has already shown circulating miRNAs to be involved in cancer through their role in controlling oncogenes and tumor suppressors. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Treatments That Limit the Expression of Trinucleotide Repeats
retrieved_docs.content: A common pathogenic mechanism in trinucleotide repeat disorders involves protein or RNA toxicity, which leads to neuronal dysfunction and death. Emerging therapies focus on reducing the expression of the mutated gene by targeting messenger RNA (mRNA) using techniques such as antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) binding and RNA interference (RNAi).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Historically, pseudogenes were referred to as "junk DNA" due to their location in non-coding sequences of the genome. However, recent studies have begun to unravel various functions of pseudogenes and their mRNA transcripts. These functions vary in regulatory roles, and they include antisense RNA, siRNA, miRNA-like, and miRNA binding or inhibiting properties.

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen is a 20-base pair, 2’-O-methoxyethyl modified antisense oligonucleotide complementary to human apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA. [3] Mipomersen aims to identify and hybridize with the human apolipoprotein B-100 mRNA via the traditional deoxyribonucleotide or ribonucleotide base pairing mechanism. The interface between the complementary mipomersen therapeutic oligonucleotide with the newly synthesized human apolipoprotein B-100 leads to the degradation of the messenger RNA. Most frequently, the hybridization of mipomersen to its target mRNA leads to the recruitment of RNase H1, a catalytic enzyme that degrades ribonucleic acid in the cellular cytoplasm. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Many neurological diseases such as spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), myotonic dystrophy, frontotemporal dementia (FTD), fragile X tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS) and Huntington's disease-like 2 (HDL-2), etc., are attributed to the expansion of non-coding RNA repeats. The repeat expansion generates abnormal hairpin folds to develop in an RNA strand, thus disrupting the normal RNA function. Accumulation of such RNA within the nucleus is termed RNA toxicity, which can induce splicing defects, nucleolar functional abnormality, cytoplasmic and mRNA transport, and autophagy. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyolysis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Gene therapy is being explored for treating inherited muscular dystrophies such as Duchenne and Becker. This includes various technologies such as read-through of early stop codons and exon skipping. Edasalonexent (CAT-1004), a nuclear factor κB (NF-κB) inhibitor that combines structural elements of salicylic acid and docosahexaenoic acid, has been evaluated in a phase 3, placebo-controlled clinical trial with some reported success. ( NCT03703882 ). Vamorolone is a glucocorticoid that stabilizes membranes by reducing lipid peroxidation and inhibiting NF-kB-mediated inflammation; it has also demonstrated functional improvement of symptoms. Many other novel treatments are being studied. [20] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Risdiplam - This drug is an oral medication that works via modifying SMN2 splicing and thereby increasing functional SMN protein levels. There have been numerous phase II/III trials that have shown efficacy with regards to improvement in motor function for patients with type II and III. Clinical trials are still ongoing and are currently under review in the US, Europe, and the UK for licensing. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Treatments That Limit the Expression of Trinucleotide Repeats
retrieved_docs.content: Several promising clinical trials are underway to identify effective methods for blocking the translation of aberrant proteins. Pharmacological approaches for treating trinucleotide repeat disorders target 3 levels of genetic expression: DNA, RNA, and protein.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: RNA sequencing has the potential to be an efficient clinical diagnostic tool because of its ability to measure gene expression outside the physiological range, allele-specific expression, and defects in alternative splicing. [18] Sequencing RNA involves isolating the RNA, preparation of an RNA library, and utilization of next-generation sequencing technology. The transcriptome can then undergo analysis for any aberrant genes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosine and acetylcholine are both classified as neurotransmitters, meaning they serve as chemical messengers that transmit signals across synapses in the nervous system. As neurotransmitters, each plays distinct but complementary roles in modulating neuronal activity, influencing processes such as sleep regulation, cardiovascular function, muscle contraction, and cognitive function. Recognizing that adenosine and acetylcholine share this broader classification helps clinicians understand their contributions to normal physiology and to various disorders, guiding therapeutic strategies that target neurotransmitter pathways for conditions like arrhythmias, neurodegenerative diseases, and sleep disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

Post‑inflammatory, postsurgical and post‑traumatic sequelae generate a spectrum of scars: post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, post‑traumatic choroid scar and choroidal‑retinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Degenerative and atrophic conditions include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, familial retinal dysplasia, age‑related choroidal degeneration, secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), partial peripapillary choroidal atrophy (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), total peripapillary choroidal atrophy (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), hereditary choroidal atrophy (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), familial choroidal dystrophy, choroidal calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and unspecified widespread retinal and choroidal inflammation. Structural complications comprise serous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 187
retrieved_docs.content: المشيمية الوراثي). Partial and complete choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect fibrotic stiffening. Focal choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and chorioretinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) illustrate localized tissue loss and scarring that may involve both choroid and retina. The choroidea serves as the anatomic substrate for additional inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, postoperative and metastatic processes, including the entities listed above, metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, familial retinal dystrophy of the choroid, and choroidal tears. Massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق) exemplifies the choroidea’s role as a site for hemorrhagic events. Collectively, these interconnected entities depict a comprehensive view of posterior‑segment ocular

Degenerative change is represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), secondary choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), hereditary choroidal calcification, calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), partial and total macular drusen, and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Vascular and hemorrhagic manifestations include choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي), clear choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق), partial and total choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc, hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and various retinal hemorrhages described above. Structural complications involve serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), general choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), and choroidal tear. Degenerative and atrophic processes comprise partial and generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal atrophy, hereditary choroidal dystrophies, partial or complete choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة) and localized choroidal plaques (بُقَعُ روث), as well as calcific and general choroidal degeneration and age‑related choroidal atrophy. Vascular disorders affecting the posterior segment include retinal artery thrombosis, retinal vein thrombosis, retinal flebitis, sub‑retinal hemorrhages ranging from focal (نزيف مشيمي) to extensive (نزيف مشيمي باثق), and various forms of choroidal detachment (serous, non‑hemorrhagic and hemorrhagic). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

Degenerative and atrophic conditions include senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), secondary spread of choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), general choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي) with partial or complete choroidal sclerosis around the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي / الكلي), partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, hereditary choroidal hemorrhage (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) and active choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق). Detachment disorders comprise serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), non‑specific choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.

Together, these entities illustrate diverse etiologies—vascular, inflammatory, traumatic, postoperative, degenerative, and genetic—that affect the choroid and retina, underscoring the importance of precise classification for diagnosis, management, and prognostication in ophthalmic care.

Degenerative changes include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, age‑related choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), secondary spreading choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), calcific choroidal degeneration (تنكس حصوي), general choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), partial and total peripapillary choroidal sclerosis (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي / الكلي), familial retinal dysplasia (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), retinal‑choroidal tear (التمزق المشيمي), and choroidal‑retinal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Hemorrhagic choroidal manifestations are noted as choroidal hemorrhage and massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق). Perivascular deposits (بُقَعُ روث) are also recorded.

Hereditary and structural abnormalities include genetic choroidal hemorrhage (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), retinal‑choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية), choroidal rupture, partial peripapillary choroidal sclerosis, total peripapillary choroidal sclerosis, hereditary choroidal sclerosis, and chorioretinal scar. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common site of hemorrhage, scar formation, dystrophy, inflammation, infection, trauma, postoperative change, and metastatic involvement. Recognizing this extensive spectrum and its hierarchical organization aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, epidemiologic tracking, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic group Dis‑orders of choroid and retina (H30‑H36) comprises a wide spectrum of choroidal and retinal pathologies that share mechanisms such as vascular compromise, inflammation, neovascular growth, degeneration, trauma, postoperative change, and systemic disease influence. Structural and degenerative entities include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, hereditary choroidal dystrophy (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), age‑related choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), peripapillary/choroidal sclerosis (partial حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي and total حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), and serous, ocular, or hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين, انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Traumatic and postoperative sequelae are represented by choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي), choroidal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1254
retrieved_docs.content: choroidal hemorrhage (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) and chorioretinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) are also identified at the choroidal level, the former suggesting underlying genetic vascular disorders and the latter reflecting prior injury or disease at the retinal‑choroidal interface. Collectively, these entities demonstrate that the choroidea serves as the common site for diverse inflammatory, scar‑forming, traumatic, postoperative, degenerative, hemorrhagic and hereditary eye conditions, underscoring its central role in the diagnosis, monitoring and management of a broad range of ocular diseases.
